---
title: "LTN25"
author: "Jeff Wu"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrreprt
---

# Causal Mechanisms

The conceptulised causal mechanism by which low-traffic neighbourhoods (LTNs) affect traffic counts and air pollution are illustrated through the directed acyclic graph (DAG) in Figure \@ref(fig-causalMechanism).

![Causal mechanism](figures/causalMechanism){#fig-causalMechanism width=80%}


# Potential Outcomes and Causal Estimands

Let $TC^{s}_{i,t}$ denote the traffic counts in neighbourhood $i$ at time $t$ under treatment status $s \in \{0,1\}$, where $s=1$ indicates the presence of a low-traffic neighbourhood intervention and $s=0$ indicates its absence. Thus, $TC^{1}_{i,t}$ represents the potential traffic counts for neighbourhood $i$ at time $t$ if a low-traffic neighbourhood intervention is implemented, while $TC^{0}_{i,t}$ represents the potential traffic counts if no such intervention is in place. 

One causal estimand that defines the causal effect of LTNs on traffic counts is the average treatment effect (ATE):
$$ ATE = E[TC^{1}_{i,t} - TC^{0}_{i,t}] $$
where the expectation, $E[\cdot]$, is taken over all neighbourhoods and time periods.

As far as this policy context is concerned,
